content.plain_english: This week your child is sorting things into groups, counting up to 10, and getting comfortable with what each number means. They are also learning to spot which group has more, which has fewer, and which two are the same.
content.in_school: Children sort buttons, beads or pasta — into colours, sizes or matching pairs. They count groups out loud and meet the words 'fewer', 'more' and 'the same'. They draw simple number lines and start to count on from any number, not just from 1.
content.home_activity: While putting clean laundry away, ask your child to sort the socks into pairs. Count how many pairs they made. Then ask whose pile has more — yours or theirs. Take turns counting on from a number you say (start at 4, count to 7). Three minutes is plenty.
dinner_table_questions: Are there more spoons or forks on the table? How do you know?
Can you start at 3 and count up to 8 with me?
Which two grapes look the same? Which one is different?

key_vocabulary.definition: Saying numbers in order to find out how many.
A straight line with numbers in order, used for counting and comparing.

example_questions: Count these 5 grapes — how many are there altogether?
Show me one more than 6 using your fingers.
Which group has more — 4 cars or 3 cars?
content.times_table_tip: Year 1 children practise counting in 2s, 5s and 10s — not yet times tables. Try counting in 2s while pairing socks: 2, 4, 6, 8.
